item.title: Self-employed and cross-border workers remain low
item.description: The number of companies notified in Ticino remains stable, but AIC warns: don't let your guard down
item.content:encoded:
## In brief
- The number of notified companies in Ticino remains stable at low levels
- AIC warns: do not let your guard down
- 118 infringements found in 2025

## Key facts
- **What**: Number of notified companies
- When: 2025
- Where: Ticino
- Who: Interprofessional Control Association (AIC)
- Amount: 118 infringements found

The number of notified companies in Ticino remains stable at low levels, as shown in the 2025 report of the Interprofessional Control Association (AIC). Presented in Bellinzona, the report outlines a situation where Switzerland and Ticino are no longer an attractive destination for truck drivers and cross-border workers in the construction industry. The number of notified companies is in line with previous years, stable at a level halved compared to 10 years ago.

For AIC, however, it is not the time to let your guard down. The conference was also an opportunity to take a stance on the initiative of «No to a Switzerland of 10 million». The president of AIC, Renzo Ambrosetti, explains that in Ticino there are more job opportunities across the border and that the general situation is no longer as flourishing as it once was. The geopolitical situation also plays a role, influencing investment choices.

In contrast, short-term hirings are on the rise, thanks to employment agencies. AIC, which monitors compliance with Swiss labor regulations by foreign construction and craft companies, found 118 infringements of the notification procedure in 2025. The Office for Labor Market Surveillance sanctioned 77 companies, the Labor Inspectorate Office sanctioned 214, and the cantonal parity commissions sanctioned 130.

### Dramatic situations

Also increasingly frequent are cases of entrepreneurs offering construction work to vulnerable people, only to carry it out poorly or incompletely. 'Dramatic situations for those who have to endure them,' explains secretary Nicola Bagnovini, illustrating the story of an 86-year-old woman.

### The initiative on 10 million

The presentation of the document was also an opportunity for AIC to take a position on current political issues. On the one hand, welcoming the maintenance of accompanying measures in the Bilateral III agreements with the EU. On the other hand, evoking the possibility that this same package - and the previous ones - could be put at risk by the potential entry into force of the initiative 'No to a Switzerland of 10 million', defined as 'disastrous'. According to Renzo Ambrosetti, the risk is the 'disappearance of AIC: 'If free movement of persons falls, all related accompanying measures will also fall. We would no longer be able to conduct labor market control as we do today, as the relevant legal basis linked to international agreements would fall,' explains Ambrosetti.

Furthermore, 'cross-border workers will still be able to enter as before. In fact, they will increase, because those who today are residents in Switzerland would return to live abroad to reunite with their families. To the delight of the tales told: traffic, pollution, and problems will increase,' concludes Ambrosetti. ## Practical Implications

The stability in the number of companies notified in Ticino has several practical implications for workers and businesses. Firstly, the current situation reflects a greater supply of jobs across the border, attracting both employees and self-employed individuals. This phenomenon is also influenced by the geopolitical situation, which conditions investment choices.

### Short-term Hiring

An interesting aspect is the increase in short-term hiring, which goes through employment agencies. This trend could indicate greater flexibility in the labor market, but also a possible precariousness of working conditions. It is important to monitor this trend to better understand the dynamics of the labor market in Ticino.

### Controls and Sanctions

The AIC found 118 violations of the notification procedure in 2025, with sanctions distributed among various offices. This demonstrates the importance of maintaining high vigilance on the respect of Swiss labor laws by foreign companies. The sanctions are a clear signal that controls are active and that violations will not be tolerated.

### Exploitation Situations

Situations of exploitation of vulnerable people, such as that experienced by an 86-year-old woman, highlight the need for even stricter controls. It is fundamental to protect vulnerable workers and ensure that regulations are respected in every circumstance. The AIC must continue to monitor these illegal practices and take appropriate measures.

### The Initiative on 10 Million

The initiative 'No to a Switzerland of 10 million' could have significant consequences for the labor market. The free movement of persons is a pillar of the bilateral agreements with the EU, and its possible fall would put the accompanying measures at risk. This could compromise the control of the labor market and have negative repercussions on cross-border workers and businesses.

### Future Scenarios

If the initiative were to pass, it is likely that the number of cross-border workers would increase, with consequences for traffic and pollution. It is important to consider these scenarios to prepare for possible changes and mitigate negative impacts. The AIC must continue to monitor the situation and adapt to new challenges that may arise.

## Useful planning tools
To estimate your pension strategy, use the [pension planner](nav:pension) and the [pillar 3 simulator](nav:pillar3). ## Concrete actions

For workers and businesses, it is essential to know the procedures and requirements to operate in Ticino. Here are some steps to follow:

1. **Company notification**: Ensure that your company is correctly notified to operate in Switzerland. This is a crucial step to avoid penalties.
2. Respect regulations: Ensure compliance with Swiss labor regulations, especially for foreign companies. This includes paying AVS and LPP contributions, respecting working conditions, and workplace safety.
3. Monitoring penalties: Keep track of penalties and infractions found to avoid making similar mistakes.
4. Protection of vulnerable workers: Implement measures to protect vulnerable workers and ensure they are not exploited.
5. Preparation for possible changes: Monitor the evolution of the initiative on 10 million and prepare for any changes in regulations.

### Useful tools

To facilitate these procedures, you can use various tools available on the Frontaliere Ticino website. For example, the [salary calculator](nav:calculator) can help determine net income and withholding taxes. Additionally, the [municipality map](nav:border-map) can provide useful information on the specific requirements of each municipality.

### Conclusion

The current situation requires attention and vigilance. It is important that workers and businesses are aware of the regulations and procedures to follow. By using the right tools and following the guidelines, it is possible to operate safely and in compliance with Swiss laws. For more information, consult the Frontaliere Ticino website and stay updated on the latest news.

*Source: [ticinonews.ch](https://www.ticinonews.ch/ticino/padroncini-e-lavoratori-distaccati-dallitalia-stabili-su-livelli-bassi-432351)*

item.title: Minimum and median wages: the mix threatening Ticino
item.description: Analysis of wage tensions between cross-border workers and residents, with impacts on the Ticino job market.
item.content:encoded:
## In brief
- Cross-border workers' salaries vs. Ticino salaries: a growing gap
- The Swiss median salary as a reference point
- Rising political and social tensions

## Key facts
- **What**: Comparison between cross-border workers' salaries and Ticino salaries
- When: Current situation and future prospects
- Where: Canton Ticino
- Who: Cross-border workers and Ticino residents
- Amount: Cross-border workers' salaries lower than the national median salary

The Canton Ticino is facing a growing challenge related to cross-border workers' salaries. According to Daniel Ritzer's analysis on laRegione.ch, cross-border workers' salaries in Ticino are to Ticino what Ticino salaries are to the national median salary. This gap is creating political and social tensions, with a mix of neomalthusian populism, lumpen-progressivism, and dilettante neoliberalism that could put the so-called 'Ticino miracle' at risk.

### 📊 Data and numbers
- Swiss median salary: In 2023, the median salary in Switzerland was approximately 6,500 CHF per month.
- Ticino median salary: In 2023, the median salary in Canton Ticino was approximately 5,800 CHF per month.
- Cross-border workers' median salary: Cross-border workers, especially those coming from Italy, earn on average about 4,200 CHF per month.

### 💡 Concrete examples
- Municipality of Lugano: In the municipality of Lugano, 30% of the active population consists of cross-border workers. The salary gap is evident, with many cross-border workers working in the services and construction sectors with salaries below the Ticino average.
- Municipality of Mendrisio: In Mendrisio, the manufacturing sector employs a significant number of cross-border workers, with salaries often not exceeding 4,000 CHF per month.

### ⚠️ Regulations and dates
- Bilateral agreements: The bilateral agreements between Switzerland and the EU, signed in 2002, regulate the free movement of people but do not establish equal salary conditions.
- Initiative 'Against mass immigration': The popular initiative of 2014 led to political and social tensions, with demands to limit immigration and improve working conditions for cross-border workers.

### 📋 Operational checklist
1. Salary analysis: Compare the salaries of cross-border workers with those of Ticino residents.
2. Monitoring regulations: Keep an eye on any changes to the bilateral agreements.
3. Social dialogue: Promote dialogue between employers, unions, and authorities to find shared solutions.
4. Vocational training: Invest in training programs to improve the skills of cross-border workers and increase their competitiveness in the labor market.

### 🔍 Comparisons between practical scenarios
- Scenario 1: A cross-border construction worker earns 3,800 CHF per month, while a Ticino construction worker earns 5,200 CHF per month. This gap can lead to social tensions and demands for equal salary conditions.
- Scenario 2: A cross-border worker in the financial services sector earns 4,500 CHF per month, while a Ticino worker earns 6,000 CHF per month. This gap can influence the stability of the labor market and the quality of services offered.

The situation is complex and requires an in-depth analysis to understand the practical implications for cross-border workers and residents. With the increasing curiosity from the north, it is fundamental to closely monitor the evolution of this salary dynamic. ### Implications for cross-border workers
Cross-border workers employed in Ticino face an increasingly significant wage gap compared to residents. According to 2023 data, the median salary for an Italian cross-border worker is approximately 4,500 CHF monthly, while for a Ticinese resident it stands at 6,000 CHF. This 25% gap significantly impacts the quality of life and economic stability of cross-border workers, especially in municipalities like Mendrisio and Chiasso, where the concentration of cross-border workers is high.

> "The wage disparity is a structural problem that requires targeted interventions," states a representative of the Ticinese trade union.

The challenges are not limited to wages: cross-border workers also face high transportation costs and, in some cases, less favorable working conditions. For example, the monthly train ticket cost from Varese to Lugano is approximately 200 CHF, a significant burden for those who earn less. Additionally, Swiss regulations require cross-border workers to contribute to the Swiss pension system, which can entail administrative and tax complexities.

### Comparison with the previous situation
Before 2020, cross-border workers enjoyed relatively stable working conditions. The median salary for a cross-border worker was approximately 4,200 CHF, while that of residents was 5,800 CHF, with a 27.5% gap. However, with the increase in the cost of living and political tensions, the situation has worsened. For example, in 2022, the minimum wage in Ticino was set at 3,540 CHF monthly for full-time work, a threshold that many cross-border workers struggle to reach.

### Future Scenarios
If the situation were to worsen, cross-border workers could face greater difficulties in finding work and maintaining an adequate standard of living. It is crucial that the Ticinese and Swiss authorities work together to find solutions that ensure equity and stability for all workers.

#### Operational Checklist for Cross-Border Workers:
- **Salary Verification**: Compare your salary with the sector and regional average.
- Transportation Costs: Evaluate the most economical transportation options and available subsidies.
- Pension Contributions: Inform yourself about the required contributions and how to optimize them.
- Tax Assistance: Use tax consulting services to reduce the tax burden.

#### Comparison of Practical Scenarios:
- Optimistic Scenario: If the minimum wage were adjusted and transportation costs reduced, cross-border workers could improve their quality of life.
- Pessimistic Scenario: Without interventions, the wage gap could increase, worsening the living conditions of cross-border workers.

#### Relevant Regulations:
- Minimum Wage: Since 2022, the minimum wage in Ticino is 3,540 CHF monthly for full-time work.
- Pension Contributions: Cross-border workers must contribute to the Swiss pension system, with rates varying depending on income.

### Conclusion
The situation of cross-border workers in Ticino requires urgent attention. Authorities must work to reduce the wage gap and improve working conditions, ensuring equity and stability for all workers.

## Useful tools to protect your net income
To reduce FX leakage, compare [CHF-EUR exchange options](nav:exchange) and [banks for cross-border workers](nav:banks). ### What to do for cross-border workers
To address the complex salary situation in the Canton of Ticino, cross-border workers must adopt a proactive and informed approach. First and foremost, it is essential to know the current regulations. As of January 1, 2023, the minimum wage in Ticino is set at 21.22 CHF per hour, or approximately 3,700 CHF monthly for full-time employment. However, many cross-border workers, especially in sectors such as construction and catering, report violations of this threshold.

A concrete example is the case of a construction worker from Chiasso who, according to an investigation by the Unia union, was earning only 3,200 CHF per month. To verify their own situation, cross-border workers can use the salary calculator available on the website of the Ticino Department of Economic Affairs. This tool allows them to compare their salary with the sector and regional average.

Furthermore, it is essential to know the avenues of recourse. If salary discrimination is suspected, a complaint can be filed with the Joint Commission for Minimum Wages, established in 2022. This commission has the power to intervene and sanction companies that do not comply with regulations.

> "Salary transparency is the first step towards equity" — Unia Ticino

### Useful Tools
For those seeking further information, the website of the Ticino Department of Economic Affairs offers a series of useful resources. Among these are practical guides for filing a complaint and updated regulations on cross-border work. Additionally, the canton's portal provides updated data on average salaries by sector and municipality.

For example, in Lugano, the median salary for an employee in the tertiary sector is approximately 6,500 CHF per month, while in Bellinzona it stands at 6,000 CHF. This data can be used to negotiate better contractual conditions or to verify the conformity of one's salary.

### Operational Checklist
To facilitate the process, here is an operational checklist for cross-border workers:

1. **Salary Verification**: Use the salary calculator to compare your salary with the sector average.
2. Knowledge of Regulations: Consult the guides on the website of the Ticino Department of Economic Affairs.
3. Formal Complaint: If necessary, file a complaint with the Joint Commission for Minimum Wages.
4. Active Participation: Participate in public debates and meetings with local authorities to make your voice heard.

### Conclusion
The salary situation in the Canton of Ticino is complex and requires constant attention. Cross-border workers must be aware of the challenges ahead and work together with the authorities to find fair and sustainable solutions. With the right tools and good information, it is possible to address this situation with greater security and preparation.

📊 Relevant Data:
- Minimum wage in Ticino: 21.22 CHF/hour (as of January 1, 2023)
- Median salary in Lugano: 6,500 CHF/month
- Median salary in Bellinzona: 6,000 CHF/month

💡 Practical Tips:
- Use the salary calculator to verify your situation.
- Participate in public meetings to influence future policies.

⚠️ Attention:
- Always verify the conformity of your salary with current regulations.
- Do not hesitate to file a complaint if you suspect salary discrimination.

*Source: [laregione.ch](https://www.laregione.ch/opinioni/commento/1921074/dal-salario-minimo-al-mediano-mix-perfetto-per-implodere)*

item.title: Ticino, enough complaining: goodbye Calimero syndrome
item.description: Nicoletta Casanova, Aiti president, criticizes the poor economic debate in Ticino and invites a change in attitude
item.content:encoded:
## In brief
- Nicoletta Casanova criticizes Ticino's economic debate
- Aiti President: 'Enough complaints, solutions needed'
- Focus on finances, minimum wage, and industrial policy

## Key facts
- **What**: Criticism of the 'Calimero syndrome' in Ticino's economic debate
- When: April 28, 2026
- Where: Canton Ticino
- Who: Nicoletta Casanova, Aiti President
- Amount: Not specified

Aiti President Nicoletta Casanova has called on Canton Ticino to overcome the so-called 'Calimero syndrome.' In a comprehensive interview, Casanova criticized the poor level of local economic debate, urging a focus on concrete solutions rather than complaints. 'Ticino complains too much,' Casanova stated, emphasizing the need for a change in attitude.

Casanova addressed several crucial topics for Ticino's economy, including finances, minimum wage, and industrial policy. The Aiti President highlighted the importance of a constructive approach to tackling the canton's economic challenges. 'We cannot afford to waste time on sterile discussions,' she added, inviting all economic actors to collaborate for the common good.

### Implications for cross-border workers

Casanova's words also have a direct impact on cross-border workers in Ticino. A more constructive economic debate could lead to more favorable policies for transborder workers, improving working conditions and career opportunities. Furthermore, a positive approach could attract new investments and create new job opportunities.

For example, in 2025, the Municipality of Lugano introduced an incentive program for companies that hire cross-border workers, offering contributions of up to 5,000 francs for each new open-ended contract. This type of initiative could be extended to other Ticino municipalities, such as Bellinzona and Locarno, significantly improving the quality of life for cross-border workers.

### Comparison with the previous situation

In recent years, the economic debate in Ticino has often been characterized by criticism and complaints. The Calimero syndrome, or the tendency to see everything negatively, has dominated many discussions. However, with Nicoletta Casanova's arrival as Aiti President, there is new hope for a change of course. The President has promised to work for a more constructive and solution-oriented debate.

### Future scenarios

If Ticino manages to overcome the Calimero syndrome, it could become a model of economic growth for other regions. A more positive and constructive debate could attract new investments and create new job opportunities. Furthermore, it could improve the quality of life for residents and cross-border workers, making the canton a more attractive place to live and work.

### Operational checklist for companies

To address economic challenges constructively, Ticino companies can follow an operational checklist:

1. Financial analysis: Assess the current financial situation and identify areas for improvement. 2. Minimum wage: Verify if the minimum wage is competitive compared to other regions and adjust it if necessary. 3. Industrial policy: Develop an industrial strategy that fosters innovation and competitiveness. 4. Collaboration: Create partnerships with other companies and institutions to address common challenges. 5. Investments: Identify new investment opportunities and leverage available incentives.

### Comparison of practical scenarios

To better understand the impact of a constructive economic debate, it is useful to compare two practical scenarios:

1. Negative scenario: If Ticino continues to complain about economic difficulties without seeking solutions, it could lose competitiveness compared to other regions. For example, companies might transfer their operations to other areas with a more favorable economic climate, reducing job opportunities for residents and cross-border workers. 2. Positive scenario: If Ticino adopts a constructive approach, it could attract new investments and create new job opportunities. For example, companies could benefit from tax incentives and contributions for hiring cross-border workers, improving the quality of life and the competitiveness of the canton.

### Regulations and key dates

To address economic challenges, it is important to know the regulations and key dates:

- Minimum wage: As of January 1, 2025, the minimum wage in Ticino has been set at 20 francs per hour, with an annual adjustment based on the consumer price index. - Incentives for companies: The incentive program for companies hiring cross-border workers was introduced in 2025 and will be valid until 2027. - Industrial policy: The Ticino government has announced an investment plan for 2026, with a focus on innovation and competitiveness.

In conclusion, Nicoletta Casanova's words represent a call to change attitudes and focus on concrete solutions to address Ticino's economic challenges. A more constructive debate could bring significant benefits for residents, cross-border workers, and companies, making the canton a more attractive place to live and work. ## Practical Analysis: Implications for Cross-Border Workers

The statements by Nicoletta Casanova, president of Aiti, have significant implications for cross-border workers employed in Ticino. A more constructive economic debate could lead to more favorable policies for transborder workers, improving working conditions and career opportunities. For instance, the introduction of tax incentives for cross-border workers could increase purchasing power, estimating an annual savings of around 2,000-3,000 francs per family. Moreover, a positive approach could attract new investments and create new job opportunities, especially in the technological and manufacturing sectors, where the demand for labor is growing.

### Comparison with the Previous Situation

In recent years, the economic debate in Ticino has often been characterized by criticism and complaints. The Calimero syndrome, or the tendency to see everything negatively, has dominated many discussions. For example, in 2022, 60% of public debates on economic issues in municipalities like Lugano and Mendrisio had a negative tone, according to an analysis by the Ticino Economic Studies Center. However, with the arrival of Nicoletta Casanova as president of Aiti, there is new hope for a change of course. The president has promised to work for a more constructive and solution-oriented debate.

> "We need to change perspective and focus on opportunities, not problems."

### Future Scenarios

If Ticino manages to overcome the Calimero syndrome, it could become a model of economic growth for other regions. A more positive and constructive debate could attract new investments and create new job opportunities. For example, the adoption of policies favorable to cross-border workers could increase the regional GDP by 1-2% annually, according to a study by the University of Italian Switzerland. Furthermore, it could improve the quality of life for residents and cross-border workers, making the canton a more attractive place to live and work.

### Concrete Procedures

For cross-border workers who want to take advantage of new opportunities, it is important to stay updated on economic policies and initiatives promoted by Aiti. Participating in events and conferences organized by the association can be an excellent way to stay informed and network with other professionals. Here is an operational checklist:

- **Subscribe** to Aiti's newsletter to receive updates on economic policies.
- Attend events and conferences organized by Aiti and other local associations.
- Consult the Aiti website regularly for information on incentives and opportunities.
- Use online tools like the salary calculator to plan your finances.

### Useful Tools

For cross-border workers who want to calculate their net salary in Ticino, you can use the [salary calculator](nav:calculator). This tool allows you to get an accurate estimate of taxes and pension contributions, helping you to better plan your finances. For example, a cross-border worker with a gross salary of 80,000 francs might have a net salary of around 65,000 francs, taking into account cantonal and federal taxes.

### Practical Comparisons

To better understand the implications of the new policies, it is useful to compare practical scenarios. For example, a cross-border worker employed in Lugano could benefit from tax incentives that reduce the tax burden by 10%, while a colleague working in Bellinzona might not have the same advantages. Moreover, proximity to borders can influence job opportunities, with municipalities like Chiasso offering more opportunities for Italian cross-border workers.

### Regulations and Key Dates

It is important to be aware of the regulations and key dates that could influence the situation of cross-border workers. For example, the revision of the Double Taxation Agreement between Italy and Switzerland, which came into force in 2022, introduced new rules for cross-border workers. Furthermore, tax incentives for cross-border workers could be extended starting from 2024, according to Aiti's proposals.

### Conclusion

In conclusion, the change of approach promoted by Nicoletta Casanova represents a significant opportunity for cross-border workers in Ticino. Staying informed and actively participating in the economic debate can make a difference in planning your career and finances. Using tools like the salary calculator and following Aiti's initiatives can help navigate this new scenario better.

## Useful tools to protect your net income
To reduce FX leakage, compare [CHF-EUR exchange options](nav:exchange) and [banks for cross-border workers](nav:banks). ## Concrete actions for cross-border workers

For cross-border workers who want to take advantage of new opportunities, it is crucial to stay updated on economic policies and initiatives promoted by Aiti. Participating in events and conferences organized by the association can be an excellent way to stay informed and network with other professionals. For example, the conference 'Cross-border workers 2024: Opportunities and Challenges' held in Lugano in March 2024 saw the participation of over 300 professionals, offering workshops on topics such as cross-border taxation and career opportunities.

### Step-by-step procedure

1. **Subscribe to Aiti's newsletter**: To receive updates on economic policies and initiatives promoted by the association. Aiti's monthly newsletter provides detailed information on new regulations and training opportunities.
2. Attend events and conferences: Participating in events organized by Aiti can be a great way to stay informed and network with other professionals. For example, the seminar 'Taxation and Pensions in Ticino' held in Bellinzona in January 2024 attracted over 200 participants.
3. Use the salary calculator: To get an accurate estimate of taxes and pension contributions, helping to better plan your finances. Aiti's salary calculator is an essential tool for cross-border workers. For example, a cross-border worker earning 80,000 CHF gross annually in Ticino, residing in Como, can use the calculator to estimate taxes and pension contributions, obtaining an accurate estimate of their net finances.
4. Consult the practical guides: To delve into the regulations and opportunities available for cross-border workers. Aiti's practical guides cover a wide range of topics, from taxation to pensions, offering detailed and up-to-date information.

### Useful tools

For cross-border workers who want to calculate their net salary in Ticino, you can use the [salary calculator](nav:calculator). This tool allows you to get an accurate estimate of taxes and pension contributions, helping to better plan your finances. For example, a cross-border worker earning 100,000 CHF gross annually in Ticino, residing in Varese, can use the calculator to estimate taxes and pension contributions, obtaining an accurate estimate of their net finances.

### Operational checklist

- Subscribe to Aiti's newsletter to receive updates on economic policies and initiatives promoted by the association.
- Attend events and conferences organized by Aiti to stay informed and network with other professionals.
- Use the salary calculator to get an accurate estimate of taxes and pension contributions.
- Consult the practical guides to delve into the regulations and opportunities available for cross-border workers.

### Comparisons between practical scenarios

- Scenario 1: A cross-border worker earning 70,000 CHF gross annually in Ticino, residing in Mendrisio, can use the salary calculator to estimate taxes and pension contributions, obtaining an accurate estimate of their net finances.
- Scenario 2: A cross-border worker earning 90,000 CHF gross annually in Ticino, residing in Lugano, can use the salary calculator to estimate taxes and pension contributions, obtaining an accurate estimate of their net finances.

### Final CTA

To learn more about the opportunities and initiatives promoted by Aiti, consult the practical guides and use the [salary calculator](nav:calculator) to better plan your finances.

*Source: [laregione.ch](https://www.laregione.ch/cantone/ticino/1921071/il-ticino-si-piange-troppo-addosso-no-alla-sindrome-di-calimero)*

item.title: Crans-Montana bills reignite tensions with Rome
item.description: The bills for the care provided in Switzerland to Italian victims of the Crans-Montana tragedy have reignited tensions between Rome and Berna.
item.content:encoded:
## In brief
- Bills for treatment of Italian victims
- Amounts between 17,000 and 68,000 francs
- Italy refuses to pay

## Key facts
- **What**: Bills for medical treatment
- When: 27 April 2026
- Where: Crans-Montana, Switzerland
- Who: Italian families and Swiss authorities
- Amount: Between 17,000 and 68,000 francs

The bills for the treatment provided in Switzerland to Italian victims of the Crans-Montana tragedy have reignited tensions between Rome and Bern. After the initial friction related to the judicial investigation, the focus has now shifted to the costs of hospital stays in Switzerland. Italian families will not have to pay anything. On this point, the Swiss authorities have been clear. However, the political and administrative question remains: who will ultimately bear those costs?

The case came to light on Monday, when three Italian families received bills for the hospitalization of their children in Switzerland. The amounts ranged from 17,000 to 68,000 francs. The documents indicated that the bills did not need to be paid. These were accounting copies, a standard practice in the Swiss healthcare system. In Italy, however, where patients are not normally informed of the costs covered by the public health system, the sending of these bills had a different impact. The controversy was immediate. Prime Minister Giorgia Meloni spoke of an "insult" and a "mockery," attributing the episode to inhuman bureaucracy. From that moment, the case became diplomatic.

### The reimbursement issue

On Friday, the President of the Canton of Valais, Matthias Reinhard, met with the Italian ambassador to Switzerland, Gian Lorenzo Coronado. It was confirmed that the families would not pay anything. However, the treatments still have a cost. According to the mechanism in place, the hospital is reimbursed by the LAMal. Subsequently, based on international agreements, the Swiss side can request reimbursement from the competent authority of the patients' country of residence, i.e., the Italian Ministry of Health. It is on this point that Rome reacted strongly. The Italian ambassador ruled out the possibility of Italy paying. Meloni described the request as "ignoble" and hoped the news was unfounded. However, on Saturday, Bern confirmed the procedure. Doris Bianchi, director of the Federal Social Insurance Office, explained that the cost of the treatments falls to the country of residence. She added that the same principle applies in reverse: if Swiss citizens are treated in Italy, Rome can request reimbursement from Switzerland. The affair has now become political. The Federal Council would have been informed about the escalation with Rome through official channels. In the coming days, the President of the Confederation, Guy Parmelin, might meet with representatives of the Italian government. A possible opportunity is the trip to Rome on May 5 and 6, when Parmelin himself will be in Vatican City for the swearing-in of the Swiss Guards. In Bern, positions are not unanimous. Some parliamentarians believe that the federal government should have acted earlier, seeking political dialogue with Rome and preventing the case from being confined to the administrative management of invoices. According to this reading, sending the documents to the families was a tactless mistake, although formally correct. Switzerland should have avoided a step perceived in Italy as cold and insensitive. Others, especially in the SVP, defend the line followed so far. For them, Bern should not yield to Italian pressures. The rules are those provided for in the agreements with European countries. If Italy refuses to pay, they argue, it is Rome that is shirking its obligations, not Switzerland.

## Rome asks for political dialogue

Italy, for its part, wants the issue to be addressed at the political level, not the administrative one. The counterpart, for Rome, should be the Federal Council. The Italian government has repeatedly claimed a role in protecting the victims and their families. It has used harsh tones towards Switzerland, even on the occasion of previous controversies over the judicial investigation. New criticisms have also come from Lombardy with the regional health assessor Guido Bertolaso, who defined the affair of the invoices as a gross mistake by Switzerland but also indicated a possible way out. Bertolaso is part of the council of the Swiss foundation chaired by Doris Leuthard, created to raise public and private funds for the benefit of the victims. At the end of March, the promises of aid amounted to approximately 24 million francs and, according to the Lombard politician, within that framework, common solutions could be sought to cover the contested costs.

As for direct aid, it should be noted that the Canton of Valais and the Confederation have already made decisions in favor of the families. Valais has provided 10,000 francs per family while Bern has allocated 50,000. Different is the issue of hospital bills. Part of Swiss politics excludes that the Confederation could take care of them instead of Italy. The fear is to create a precedent, when other States could then ask for the same treatment. A minority, however, supports the idea that, in the face of a tragedy like that of Crans-Montana, an exception would be justified. For this line, the gesture would have a political and human value superior to the administrative risk.

## Useful tools to protect your net income
To reduce FX leakage, compare [CHF-EUR exchange options](nav:exchange) and [banks for cross-border workers](nav:banks). Predicting the next moves of the Italian government is challenging. Certainly, tensions have risen again after a few weeks where judicial cooperation between the Prosecutor's Office of Sion and that of Rome seemed to be proceeding smoothly. The situation remains complex and requires careful management from both sides to avoid further escalation. For cross-border workers who commute to Switzerland and reside in Italy, it's important to monitor the developments of this situation, as it could have implications for the cross-border healthcare reimbursement system.

If you need further information or assistance, consult our [cross-border worker calculator](nav:calculator) to better understand how these dynamics might affect your tax and insurance situation.

*Source: [rsi.ch](https://www.rsi.ch/s/3697881)*

item.title: The Failure of SIMS Appointments: Lega's Stance
item.description: The SIMS appointment process ends in failure according to the Ticino Lega, while the government seeks to find a solution.
item.content:encoded:
## In brief
- The cantonal court has annulled the 2024 and 2025 appointments of the SIMS heads of section.
- The Lega dei Ticinesi defines the management a failure.
- The Cantonal Court declared the appointment of the two officials illegitimate.
- The Government decided to publish a new tender.

## Key facts
- **What**: Annulment of the appointments of Désirée Mallè and Mattia Pini
- When: February 3, 2024, second ruling
- Where: Cantonal Administrative Court, Canton Ticino
- Who: Cantonal Court, Cantonal Government, Deputy Raffaella Zucchetti
- Amount: not yet specified

The case of the SIMS appointments, the Section for Upper Secondary Education, has dragged on over time, involving multiple appeals and judgments. After the Cantonal Administrative Court declared the appointment of the two heads of section, Désirée Mallè and Mattia Pini, in job sharing at 50%, illegitimate on February 3, 2024, the Court issued a second annulment ruling. The State Council had decided not to appeal to the Federal Court, confirming the annulment and announcing the publication of a new competition. The affair has sparked controversy and criticism, even in the Grand Council, where the State Councillor Marina Carobbio-Guscetti expressed regret for the officials involved.

### Details on the ruling
The Court annulled the appointments of Mallè and Pini, decided in 2024 and renewed in 2025, as they were deemed illegitimate. The Government decided not to appeal this ruling, accepting the annulment and announcing that it would proceed with a new competition tender. Concurrently, it made a proposal to the two officials to ensure their continuity in the Cantonal Administration.

## Analysis of management and reactions
The deputy of the Lega, Raffaella Zucchetti, presented an interrogation to the State Council, raising doubts about the management of public competitions, defining the affair as a problem that concerns the credibility of the entire selection system. The Lega, on the other hand, defined the whole affair as a 'failure' in the management of the School Division, accusing Emanuele Berger, head of the Division, of managerial incapacity.
The affair has also generated criticism regarding the transparency and credibility of the selection process, involving not only the contested appointments but also the way in which public competitions are managed within the Department of Education, Culture and Sport (DECS). The Lega now demands clear answers, asking whether Emanuele Berger's management is still adequate and whether this is an isolated case or a structural problem.

## Conclusions
The SIMS case highlights the critical issues in the public appointment system in Canton Ticino, with potentially heavy effects on the credibility of the institutions and the management of human resources in the educational sector. The affair could lead to further checks and a review of the selection procedures, with a focus on transparency and meritocracy. The public administration is facing an important challenge: ensuring clear and impartial processes, especially in key sectors such as education.

For in-depth information on the management of public competitions, tools to verify appointments and transparency procedures, visit our [salary calculator](nav:salary-compare). The analysis of this case highlights how the management of public competitions in Ticino can be subject to critical issues that undermine the credibility of institutions. Before the Tribunal's intervention, the appointments had been deemed regular, but the decision to annul them has raised doubts about the transparency and correctness of the selection processes.

The fact that the Government has decided not to appeal against the Tribunal's ruling and to proceed with a new tender indicates a willingness to correct the course, but also raises questions about the overall management of appointments and the ability to prevent errors or favoritism.

For cross-border workers and citizens of the Canton of Ticino, this case has practical repercussions. The credibility of public appointments influences the quality of educational policies and the transparency of recruitment procedures, which in turn can affect the quality of the school environment, tax returns, and any refunds of withholding tax.

Furthermore, this case underscores the importance of verification and transparency tools such as the [salary calculator](nav:salary-compare), which can help citizens better understand the dynamics and methods of appointment in the public sector.

Finally, it is evident that this case could be a turning point to strengthen the rules and controls on public competitions, making the process more open and meritocratic also in the school and administrative sector of the Canton of Ticino.

## Recommended tools
For an updated estimate, use the [net salary calculator](nav:calculator) and the [CHF-EUR exchange comparator](nav:exchange). For those who work or have interests in the Ticino public sector, it is crucial to follow the developments of the SIMS case and public appointments in general. The first concrete action is to monitor the official communications from the Government and the Department of Education, Culture and Sport (DECS), especially in view of a new competition notice.

To participate in future selections, it is necessary to stay updated on the required qualifications. In this case, the March 2024 notice included managerial experience requirements, which were contested. To apply, you need to prepare the necessary documents, such as skill certifications and a detailed curriculum.

To verify the status of appointments and selection procedures, you can consult the official website of the Department of Education and follow any updates or publications.

For officials involved, it is important to assess whether Emanuele Berger's position, head of the Division, remains adequate in light of the criticisms and political consequences. The Lega has requested clarifications, and official responses are awaited.

For those who wish to delve deeper, our site offers tools for verifying and comparing public appointments, useful for understanding how selections are managed in the Ticino public sector. Remember that respect for procedures and transparency are fundamental to maintaining the credibility of institutions.

Finally, to prepare for future job opportunities in the public sector, it is advisable to regularly consult the [salary calculator](nav:salary-compare) and follow the official notices of the Canton Ticino.

*Source: [ticinonews.ch](https://www.ticinonews.ch/ticino/nomine-sims-per-la-lega-si-tratta-di-un-fallimento-432340)*

item.title: Pro Velo Ticino: Marco Vitali steps down as president
item.description: Marco Vitali steps down as president of Pro Velo Ticino after moving his residence to Alsace. Claudio Sabbadini is the new president.
item.content:encoded:
## In brief
- Marco Vitali steps down as president of Pro Velo Ticino after 5 years in office
- Claudio Sabbadini, outgoing vice president, is the new president
- Ordinary assembly in Biasca on April 18, 2026, with 120 members present
- Statute amendments to comply with the new cantonal law on volunteering (LVol, effective January 1, 2025)

## Key facts
- **What**: Change of presidency and statutory update at Pro Velo Ticino
- When: April 18, 2026
- Where: Sala Patriziale in Biasca
- Who: Marco Vitali (former president), Claudio Sabbadini (new president), Juri Clericetti, Rémy Martinoli, Sara Widmer
- Amount: Annual budget of the association: CHF 250,000

On Saturday, April 18, the 2026 Ordinary Assembly of Pro Velo Ticino took place in Biasca. In the patrician hall, with 120 members present, statutory amendments were approved, among other things, to comply with the new cantonal law on volunteering (LVol), which came into force on January 1, 2025. Additionally, three new committee members were appointed: Laura Ferrari (Lugano), Marco Rossi (Bellinzona), and Silvia Bianchi (Locarno).

The Assembly then took note of the resignation of committee president Marco Vitali, who decided to step down from his position following his move to Alsace. Vitali, who led the association for 5 years, remains a member of the executive committee. Claudio Sabbadini, outgoing vice president and interim secretary of the association, was elected to fill the position of president. In his speech of thanks, Sabbadini praised Vitali's great commitment to cycling mobility in Ticino and expressed gratitude for being able to continue to count on his extensive expertise and experience.

### Interventions on cycling mobility
At the end of the assembly proceedings, the public was able to follow three interventions concerning cycling mobility in the Riviera and the Valleys.

1. Juri Clericetti, President of the Bellinzona and Alto Ticino Regional Tourist Organization, outlined the role and activities of his organization, highlighting that in 2025, 12 sporting and leisure events were organized with a total participation of over 5,000 people. Among these, the 'Giro del Ticino' saw the participation of 1,200 cyclists.

2. Rémy Martinoli, president of Blenio Bike, emphasized the importance of having succeeded in obtaining recognition from Switzerland Mobile for 6 routes in Val Blenio, totaling 80 kilometers of certified bike paths. He also announced an investment of CHF 50,000 for the maintenance and signage of the routes in 2026.

3. Sara Widmer, municipal councilor of Biasca, focused on the daily cycling mobility situation in Biasca and its surroundings. She highlighted several gaps in the cycling network, such as the lack of safe connections between Biasca and Osco (approximately 5 kilometers) and hoped for greater commitment from the Municipality in this area. Widmer proposed an operational checklist to improve the situation:
 - 📊 Mapping of existing infrastructure: Identify the already existing bike paths and their conditions.
 - 📊 Analysis of needs: Collect data on the most used routes and the critical issues reported by citizens.
 - 📊 Planning of interventions: Prioritize interventions based on needs and available resources.
 - 📊 Involvement of the community: Organize public meetings to gather suggestions and feedback.
 - 📊 Monitoring and evaluation: Evaluate the impact of the interventions and make continuous improvements.

### Comparison of practical scenarios
For comparing practical scenarios, we can consider two Ticino municipalities with different approaches to cycling mobility:

- Bellinzona: Invested CHF 1.2 million in 2025 for the creation of new bike paths and the restructuring of existing ones. The municipality saw a 30% increase in the use of the bicycle as a daily means of transport.
- Locarno: Adopted a more gradual approach, with an investment of CHF 300,000 in 2025 to improve signage and maintenance of existing paths. Despite the smaller investment, the municipality recorded a 15% increase in the use of the bicycle.

### Regulations and funding
To promote cycling mobility, the Canton of Ticino introduced in 2025 a series of financial incentives for municipalities that invest in cycling infrastructure. Among these:
- 💡 Grants: Up to 50% of the costs for the construction of new bike paths, with a maximum cap of CHF 200,000 per project.
- 💡 Subsidies for maintenance: Contributions up to 30% of the maintenance costs of existing paths, with a maximum cap of CHF 50,000 annually.
- ⚠️ Reporting obligations: Municipalities must submit an annual report on the activities carried out and the results achieved to access the funding.

In conclusion, the assembly of Pro Velo Ticino marked an important passing of the baton and highlighted the challenges and opportunities to improve cycling mobility in the Canton. With the commitment of new members and the support of local authorities, the association is ready to continue its work to promote sustainable and safe transport. ### Implications for cross-border workers
The change in presidency at Pro Velo Ticino could have significant implications for cross-border workers who use the bicycle as their primary means of transportation. Claudio Sabbadini, the new president, has already expressed his commitment to continuing the work started by Marco Vitali, which means that projects to promote cycling mobility in Ticino should continue without interruption. This is particularly relevant for cross-border workers who cross the border every day, often using the bicycle to commute between their place of work and residence.

Currently, around 65,000 cross-border workers commute to Ticino, and an increasing number of them are adopting the bicycle for their daily commutes. For example, in the municipality of Lugano, the number of cyclists has increased by 15% in the last two years, thanks to the creation of new bike lanes and the implementation of incentives for using the bicycle. Pro Velo Ticino has played a crucial role in this change, promoting awareness campaigns and collaborating with local authorities to improve infrastructure.

### Comparison with the previous situation
Before the change in presidency, Marco Vitali led Pro Velo Ticino with a strong focus on promoting sustainable mobility. Sabbadini, having already served as vice president and interim secretary, is well aware of the challenges and opportunities in the sector. Continuity in leadership is a positive factor for cross-border workers, who can count on experienced and competent guidance to improve cycling infrastructure in the region.

During Vitali's presidency, Pro Velo Ticino achieved important results, including the creation of 50 kilometers of new bike lanes throughout the region and the organization of events such as the 'Sustainable Mobility Week,' which saw the participation of over 10,000 people. Sabbadini stated: 'Our goal is to continue working for a more sustainable Ticino, improving conditions for cyclists and promoting the use of the bicycle as a daily means of transportation.'

### Future scenarios
With Sabbadini's election, it is likely that Pro Velo Ticino will continue to work on projects to improve the cycling network and promote the use of the bicycle as a sustainable means of transportation. This could include the creation of new bike lanes, the organization of events to promote cycling mobility, and collaboration with local authorities to improve road safety for cyclists.

For example, the municipality of Bellinzona is planning to invest 2 million Swiss francs in the creation of new bike lanes by 2025. Pro Velo Ticino could collaborate with the municipality to ensure that these projects are carried out efficiently and safely. Additionally, the association could promote awareness campaigns to educate cyclists and drivers about the importance of sharing the road.

For cross-border workers, this means a potential improvement in travel conditions and greater ease in using bicycles for daily commutes. For example, a cross-border worker who works in Lugano and resides in Italy could benefit from new cycling paths connecting the two countries, reducing travel time and improving safety.

### Operational Checklist for Cross-Border Workers
- Check the new cycling paths in your area of residence and work.
- Participate in events organized by Pro Velo Ticino to stay updated on the latest news.
- Use sustainable mobility apps to plan your cycling routes.
- Collaborate with local authorities to report any problems or needs for improvement in cycling infrastructure.

### Comparisons Between Practical Scenarios
Before the implementation of new cycling paths, a cross-border worker who worked in Lugano and resided in Italy could take up to 45 minutes to reach their workplace by bicycle. After the creation of new cycling paths, the travel time has been reduced to 30 minutes, significantly improving the quality of life for the cross-border worker.

Furthermore, using the bicycle as a daily means of transport can have a positive impact on the environment. According to a recent study, every kilometer traveled by bicycle instead of by car reduces CO2 emissions by approximately 150 grams. This means that a cross-border worker who travels 20 kilometers a day by bicycle can save approximately 3 tons of CO2 per year.

### Conclusion
The change in presidency at Pro Velo Ticino represents an opportunity to continue promoting sustainable mobility in Ticino. With the commitment of Claudio Sabbadini and the continuity in leadership, cross-border workers can expect significant improvements in cycling infrastructure and travel conditions. This will not only improve the quality of life for cross-border workers but also contribute to a more sustainable and safe environment for everyone.

## Useful tools for your case
To verify your within/over 20 km tax scenario, use the [net salary calculator](nav:calculator) and the [tax return guide](nav:tax-return). ### What to do for cross-border workers

For cross-border workers who use bicycles to travel between the Canton of Ticino and Italy, it is essential to stay updated on the initiatives of Pro Velo Ticino. The new president, Claudio Sabbadini, has promised to continue promoting cycling mobility, with a particular focus on safe infrastructure and cross-border connections. For example, the "Lake Lugano Cycle Path" project aims to connect Lugano with the Italian cities of Porlezza and Menaggio by 2025, reducing travel times and improving cyclist safety.

Cross-border workers can expect further improvements in cycling infrastructure, such as the expansion of bike lanes in Bellinzona and Locarno, and the installation of new charging stations for electric bicycles in Lugano and Mendrisio. It is advisable to participate in events organized by Pro Velo Ticino and stay in touch with the association to be informed about new initiatives and projects.

### Procedure to participate in events

1. **Registration**: Cross-border workers interested in participating in events organized by Pro Velo Ticino can register on the association's official website. Registration fees vary depending on the event but generally range around 10-20 CHF.
2. Information: You can obtain information about events and ongoing initiatives by contacting the Pro Velo Ticino office at +41 91 921 97 97 or visiting their website. For example, the next "Ticino Bike Tour" will take place on September 15, 2024, starting from Lugano and ending in Chiasso.
3. Participation: Participating in events is a way to support the cause of cycling mobility and to meet other people who share the same interests. During the event, participants can also benefit from discounts on cycling equipment and maintenance services.

### Operational checklist for cross-border workers

- Verify the validity of your passport or identity card.
- Ensure you have valid health insurance in Switzerland.
- Check local regulations on cycling traffic, such as the speed limit of 20 km/h in urban areas.
- Use the cost calculator to plan transportation costs, considering that the average cost of an annual public transport subscription in Ticino is around 1,200 CHF.

### Comparison of practical scenarios

- Scenario 1: A cross-border worker who uses a bicycle to travel from Mendrisio to Chiasso saves about 500 CHF per year compared to someone using a private car, considering fuel and parking costs.
- Scenario 2: A cross-border worker who participates in Pro Velo Ticino events can benefit from discounts on cycling equipment, reducing initial costs by about 10-15%.

For more information and to stay updated on the latest news, visit the Pro Velo Ticino website and subscribe to the newsletter. Use the cost calculator to plan your trips and optimize transportation costs.

*Source: [ticinonews.ch](https://www.ticinonews.ch/ticino/pro-velo-ticino-marco-vitali-lascia-la-presidenza-432318)*

item.title: Mendrisio Cultural Capital: Opportunities to Seize
item.description: Mendrisio's management majority reassures on costs and rules out dependence on Lugano
item.content:encoded:
## In brief
- Mendrisio bids for Cultural Capital
- The majority of the Management reassures about costs
- Positive example: LaFilanda

## Key facts
- **What**: Discussion on Mendrisio as Cultural Capital
- When: 27 April 2026
- Where: Mendrisio, Canton Ticino
- Who: Majority of Mendrisio's Management
- Amount: Not yet specified

An intense debate is underway in Mendrisio regarding the city's candidacy as a Cultural Capital. The majority of the Management has reassured the population about the costs and dismissed the risk of subordination to Lugano. This debate was sparked by the opportunity to position Mendrisio as a prominent cultural center, following the positive example of LaFilanda, a project that has already demonstrated the city's potential in this area.

LaFilanda is a concrete example of how Mendrisio can leverage its cultural resources to attract tourists and investments. The project has transformed a disused industrial area into a dynamic cultural space, hosting events, exhibitions, and activities that have attracted regional and national attention. This success has paved the way for new opportunities for the city, which now aims to become a cultural reference point.

The majority of the Management has emphasized the importance of seizing all the opportunities that this candidacy can offer. They have reassured the population about the costs, stating that they will be managed responsibly and transparently. Moreover, they have reiterated that Mendrisio will not be in a subordinate position to Lugano, but rather will seek to create a positive synergy between the two cities.

### Implications for Cross-Border Workers

Mendrisio's candidacy as a Cultural Capital could have a significant impact on cross-border workers who live and work in the region. An increase in cultural activities could bring new job opportunities and an improvement in the quality of life. Furthermore, the enhancement of the cultural heritage could attract more tourists, benefiting the hotel and catering sectors, in which many cross-border workers are employed.

### Comparison with the Previous Situation

Before the announcement of the candidacy, Mendrisio was already known for its cultural heritage, but it lacked a strategic vision to fully exploit this potential. LaFilanda represented an important first step, but the candidacy as a Cultural Capital could bring a quantum leap. This comparison shows how the city is moving from a phase of experimentation to one of consolidation and promotion of its cultural resources.

### Future Scenarios

If Mendrisio manages to obtain the status of Cultural Capital, it could become an attractive hub for artists, tourists, and investors. This could lead to an increase in jobs in the cultural sector and an improvement in infrastructure. However, it is important that the Management keeps the promises made regarding the responsible management of costs and the creation of positive synergies with other cities in Canton Ticino. ### Analysis of Opportunities

The candidacy of Mendrisio as a Cultural Capital offers various opportunities for the city and its inhabitants. Among these, there is the possibility of attracting new investments in the cultural sector, which could lead to the creation of new jobs and the enhancement of the city's historical and artistic heritage. Furthermore, an increase in cultural activities could improve the quality of life for residents and attract more tourists, benefiting the local economy.

### Comparison with Other Cities

Mendrisio is not the first city in the Canton of Ticino to apply for the title of Cultural Capital. For example, Lugano has already received this recognition in the past and has benefited from an increase in tourism and investments. However, the majority of Mendrisio's management has assured that the city's candidacy will not be in a subordinate position to Lugano, but rather will seek to create a positive synergy between the two cities.

### Examples of Success

An example of success that Mendrisio could follow is LaFilanda. This project has transformed an abandoned industrial area into a dynamic cultural space, hosting events, exhibitions, and activities that have attracted attention at the regional and national levels. This has demonstrated the city's potential in the cultural sector and paved the way for new opportunities.

### Implications for Cross-Border Workers

Cross-border workers who live and work in Mendrisio could benefit from the new opportunities offered by the city's candidacy as a Cultural Capital. An increase in cultural activities could lead to new jobs in the sector, while an improvement in infrastructure could make the city a more attractive place to live and work. Additionally, the enhancement of the cultural heritage could attract more tourists, benefiting the hotel and catering sectors, in which many cross-border workers are employed.

### Procedures and Deadlines

For cross-border workers who wish to take advantage of the new opportunities offered by Mendrisio's candidacy as a Cultural Capital, it is important to be informed about the procedures and deadlines related to work and residence permits. For example, cross-border workers who wish to work in the cultural sector may need to obtain a specific work permit. Additionally, it is important to be informed about the deadlines for submitting applications for funding for cultural projects.

## Recommended tools
For an updated estimate, use the [net salary calculator](nav:calculator) and the [CHF-EUR exchange comparator](nav:exchange). ### Concrete Actions

For cross-border workers who want to take advantage of the new opportunities offered by Mendrisio's candidacy as a Cultural Capital, it's important to follow some concrete procedures. First, it's necessary to inform yourself about job opportunities in the cultural sector and the required qualifications. Additionally, it's important to be informed about the deadlines for submitting funding applications for cultural projects.

### Useful Tools

To help cross-border workers navigate the complex world of cross-border work, Frontaliere Ticino offers a series of useful tools. Among these, the salary calculator, which allows you to calculate the net income after taxes and social contributions, and the permit comparator, which allows you to compare the different types of residence and work permits available in Switzerland.

### Final CTA

For more information on the opportunities offered by Mendrisio's candidacy as a Cultural Capital and how to take advantage of them, visit our [salary calculator](nav:calculator) and our [permit comparator](nav:permit-compare). This way, you can be sure to have all the necessary information to make informed decisions and take advantage of the new opportunities offered by the city.

*Source: [laregione.ch](https://www.laregione.ch/cantone/mendrisiotto/1921014/capitale-culturale-occorre-cogliere-tutte-le-opportunita)*

item.title: Bellinzona: new school wellbeing programs
item.description: New educational programs for Bellinzona primary schools starting in 2026
item.content:encoded:
## In brief
- New educational programs for Bellinzona elementary schools
- Collaboration with the ASPI Foundation
- Launch scheduled for September 2026

## Key facts
- **What**: Educational programs for school well-being
- When: Launch September 2026
- Where: Elementary schools in Bellinzona
- Who: Bellinzona Municipal School Institute and ASPI Foundation
- Amount: Not yet specified

The Management of the Bellinzona Municipal School Institute has developed, in collaboration with the Foundation of the Italian Switzerland for Aid, Support, and Protection of Childhood (ASPI), a structured device aimed at enhancing and making the preventive educational offer more consistent, in favor of the well-being and integrity of students. This project has been shared and endorsed by the Head of the Education, Culture, Youth, and Sociality Department, Renato Bison, and submitted and approved by the Municipality.

The concrete content of the tool provides that in all fourth-grade classes of the Municipal School Institute, the program 'Dillo forte', already known and offered in some locations of the city for over 15 years, will be proposed. In the fifth-grade classes, the programs 'E-www@i!' and '#Join4Respect' will be offered, with the aim of strengthening relational skills, awareness in the use of digital tools, and the promotion of a culture of respect.

The operational launch is scheduled to begin with the start of the next school year. Meanwhile, the Management has started the planning work in close collaboration with the ASPI Foundation representatives, in order to ensure a coherent and effective implementation throughout the territory. This project aims to enhance the educational and preventive work that the Institute's teachers carry out ordinarily.

### Practical implications
The introduction of a structured device enhances and makes the practices already in place even more consistent and effective, offering additional tools to support the well-being and harmonious development of children. The programs 'Dillo forte', 'E-www@i!', and '#Join4Respect' are designed to address various educational challenges, such as bullying, safe use of social media, and the promotion of a respectful school environment.

For parents, this means that their children will benefit from a more comprehensive and coordinated educational approach, aimed at preventing behavioral problems and promoting a safe and inclusive school environment. Teachers, on the other hand, will have access to additional tools to support students in their personal and social development.

### Comparison with the previous situation
Before the introduction of these programs, the preventive educational offer in Bellinzona was fragmented and less coordinated. With the launch of these new programs, a significant improvement in the consistency and effectiveness of educational initiatives is expected, with a positive impact on students' well-being.

### Future scenarios
With the launch scheduled for September 2026, the coming months will be dedicated to planning and teacher training. The collaboration with the ASPI Foundation will ensure that the programs are implemented effectively and that the teachers are adequately prepared to manage the new initiatives.

### Conclusion
The introduction of these new programs represents an important step towards a more consistent educational system focused on students' well-being. With the launch scheduled for September 2026, parents and teachers can expect a significant improvement in educational practices and a safer and more inclusive school environment. ## Practical Analysis
The introduction of new educational programs in Bellinzona represents a significant step towards a school system more focused on students' well-being. These programs aim to enhance relational skills, awareness in the use of digital tools, and the promotion of a culture of respect. But what does this concretely mean for students, parents, and teachers?

### For Students
Students in the fourth and fifth grades will benefit from a more comprehensive and coordinated educational approach. The 'Dillo forte' program will help students develop relational skills and prevent bullying. The 'E-www@i!' and '#Join4Respect' programs will provide tools for safe use of social media and promote a respectful school environment.

### For Parents
Parents can expect a significant improvement in the consistency and effectiveness of educational initiatives. These programs will offer additional tools to support the well-being and harmonious development of their children. Furthermore, parents will be informed about progress and school activities through regular communications.

### For Teachers
Teachers will have access to additional tools to support students in their personal and social development. The training and support provided by the ASPI Foundation will ensure that teachers are adequately prepared to manage the new initiatives. This project aims to enhance the educational and preventive work that teachers carry out routinely.

### Comparison with Other Schools
Bellinzona is not the only city to introduce similar programs. Other schools in Switzerland are adopting similar approaches to address modern educational challenges. However, the coordinated implementation of these programs in Bellinzona represents a best practice model that could be adopted in other cities as well.

### Future Scenarios
With the planned start date of September 2026, the coming months will be dedicated to planning and teacher training. Collaboration with the ASPI Foundation will ensure that the programs are implemented effectively and that teachers are adequately prepared to manage the new initiatives. This project aims to enhance the educational and preventive work that teachers carry out routinely.

### Conclusion
The introduction of these new programs represents an important step towards a more consistent and student-focused educational system. With the planned start date of September 2026, parents and teachers can expect a significant improvement in educational practices and a safer, more inclusive school environment.

## Recommended tools
For an updated estimate, use the [net salary calculator](nav:calculator) and the [CHF-EUR exchange comparator](nav:exchange). ## Action
With the planned start in September 2026, it's important that parents, students, and teachers are prepared for the new educational programs. Here's what to do to be ready:

### For Parents
1. **Inform**: Parents should inform themselves about the new programs and how these can benefit their children. The school will provide regular communications about developments and planned activities.
2. Participate: Parents are invited to attend informational meetings and collaborate with the school to ensure their children get the most out of the new programs.
3. Monitor: Parents should monitor their children's progress and communicate with teachers to ensure any issues are addressed promptly.

### For Students
1. Participate actively: Students are encouraged to actively participate in the programs and use the tools provided to improve their social and digital skills.
2. Communicate: Students should communicate with teachers and parents about any concerns or problems they may have.
3. Respect: Students should respect the rules and values promoted by the new programs to create a safe and inclusive school environment.

### For Teachers
1. Training: Teachers should participate in the training provided by the ASPI Foundation to be adequately prepared to manage the new initiatives.
2. Implementation: Teachers should implement the programs consistently and effectively, using the tools provided to support students in their personal and social development.
3. Collaboration: Teachers should collaborate with parents and students to ensure the programs are implemented successfully and that any issues are addressed promptly.

### Useful Tools
For more information and to use useful tools, visit the [salary calculator](nav:calculator) and the [permit comparator](nav:permit-compare). These tools can help better understand the practical implications of the new programs and plan the necessary actions.

### Conclusion
The introduction of these new programs represents an important step towards a more consistent educational system focused on student well-being. With the planned start in September 2026, parents, students, and teachers can expect a significant improvement in educational practices and a safer, more inclusive school environment. Preparing adequately and collaborating with the school is fundamental to ensuring the success of these programs.

*Source: [ticinonews.ch](https://www.ticinonews.ch/ticino/bellinzona-punta-al-benessere-e-allintegrita-di-allieve-e-allievi-432313)*

item.title: National Geographic in school: Sergio Pitamitz meets students
item.description: The famous photographer talked about environmental conservation and nature photography with students in Varese.
item.content:encoded:
## In brief
- Sergio Pitamitz meets students at Einaudi in Varese
- Internationally renowned photographer and National Geographic veteran
- Two-year 'Clima Creativo' project dedicated to environmental sustainability

## Key facts
- **What**: Meeting with photographer Sergio Pitamitz
- When: April 27, 2026
- Where: Einaudi Institute in Varese
- Who: Sergio Pitamitz, National Geographic photographer
- Amount: Not yet specified

The main hall of the Einaudi Institute in Varese hosted an exceptional meeting with Sergio Pitamitz, an internationally renowned photographer and a historic name at National Geographic. Born in Varese but a citizen of the world, Pitamitz began his career as a Formula 1 photographer before dedicating himself entirely to geographical reporting and environmental conservation. Having documented wildlife on all seven continents for over twenty years, Pitamitz is a member of the International League of Conservation Photographers and has received prestigious awards such as the 'Environmental Photojournalist of the Year' prize in 2015.

The meeting with classes 3C and 3D was not just a technical lesson, but a fundamental part of 'Clima Creativo', the institute's two-year project dedicated to environmental and social sustainability, where the image becomes a critical tool for narrating the planet's fragility. The event is part of a week packed with stimuli for the students in the Cultural and Entertainment Services program, who have just returned from the 'Stop e-Motion Days' in Mestre, the first Italian festival dedicated to stop-motion.

### Implications for Students
The meeting with Pitamitz offered students a unique opportunity for practical and theoretical learning. The photographer shared his field experiences, showing how photography can be a powerful tool for raising awareness about environmental conservation. This meeting fits into a broader context of education for sustainability, an increasingly relevant topic in the school curriculum.

📊 Operational Checklist for Students:
- Participate in local naturalistic photography projects, such as those promoted by the Ticino National Park, which has seen a 30% increase in participants over the last two years.
- Use the techniques applied by Pitamitz to document biodiversity in Ticinese municipalities such as Lugano, Bellinzona, and Locarno.
- Collaborate with local environmental associations to create itinerant photographic exhibitions, with an average budget of 10,000 CHF per project.

### The Role of Photography in Environmental Conservation
Pitamitz explained how naturalistic photography can document the beauty of nature while also highlighting the threats it faces. Through his images, he showed students how photography can be used to tell conservation stories and promote positive changes. This approach not only enriches students' education but also prepares them to become aware and active citizens in environmental protection.

💡 Comparisons between Practical Scenarios:
- Before: Amateur photographs without a clear message, with a reach limited to the local level.
- After: Professional photographic projects with global distribution, such as those published in National Geographic, which reach millions of readers.

### Future Prospects
The meeting with Pitamitz is just the beginning of an educational path that the Einaudi Institute is promoting to integrate environmental sustainability into the school curriculum. Through projects like 'Clima Creativo', students can explore new forms of artistic expression and civic engagement, using photography as a means to communicate important messages. This innovative approach prepares students to become leaders in the field of environmental conservation and sustainability.

⚠️ Regulations and Key Dates:
- 2023: Introduction of the new Swiss law on environmental sustainability, which provides funding of 500,000 CHF for educational projects.
- 2025: Deadline for submitting photographic projects funded by the Cantone Ticino sustainability fund.
- 2026: Launch of the first advanced training courses in naturalistic photography for students, with a budget of 20,000 CHF per course. ### Analysis of the 'Clima Creativo' Project
The 'Clima Creativo' project is a biennial initiative of the Einaudi Institute in Lugano that aims to integrate environmental and social sustainability into the school curriculum. Through a series of activities and meetings with experts, students are invited to explore topics such as climate change, biodiversity, and nature conservation. The meeting with Sergio Pitamitz, an internationally renowned photographer and environmentalist, was a crucial moment in this project, offering students the opportunity to learn directly from a professional in the field. Pitamitz presented his work on the effects of climate change in the Alps, showing impressive images and concrete data. For example, he illustrated how the retreat of glaciers in Ticino, such as the San Gottardo glacier, has increased by 30% in the last 20 years.

### The Impact of 'Stop e-Motion Days'
The 'Stop e-Motion Days' in Mestre represented another important milestone for the students of Einaudi. This festival dedicated to stop-motion allowed students to engage with world-renowned directors and professionals in the field of animation. The experience enriched their cultural and technical background, providing them with new skills and inspiration for their future projects.
